sections.section_title: 4.1 Does an Electric Current Have a Magnetic Effect?

content.text: When electric current flows through a conductor (like a wire), it produces a magnetic field around it. This phenomenon is known as the magnetic effect of electric current. The magnetic field disappears when the current stops flowing. This discovery was made by Hans Christian Oersted in 1820, who noticed that whenever an electrical circuit was closed or opened, the needle of a magnetic compass lying nearby deflected.
The region around a magnet or a current-carrying wire where its magnetic effect can be felt (such as by the deflection of a compass needle) is said to have a magnetic field.
The connection between electricity and magnetism forms the basis of many practical applications, such as devices like electromagnets, electric bells, motors, fans, and loudspeakers.

To demonstrate the magnetic effect of electric current: Place a magnetic compass beneath a wire carrying electric current. When current flows, the compass needle deflects from its original direction. When the current stops, the needle returns to its original position.

A current-carrying coil that behaves as a magnet is called an electromagnet. When electric current flows through a coil of wire, the coil produces a magnetic field. Inserting an iron core inside the coil makes the electromagnet significantly stronger.
For practical applications, most electromagnets have an iron core to make them stronger. The iron core amplifies the magnetic field produced by the coil.

Just like a bar magnet, an electromagnet also has two poles—North and South. The polarity of an electromagnet can be determined using a magnetic compass; unlike poles (North-South) attract each other.

The strength of an electromagnet can be changed by: (1) Changing the amount of electric current flowing through the coil—more current produces a stronger magnetic field; (2) Changing the number of turns in the coil—more turns produce a stronger magnetic field; (3) Using both methods simultaneously. The poles of an electromagnet can be reversed by changing the direction of the electric current.

If you use a battery with more cells (2 cells instead of 1), it provides a larger current, creating a stronger magnetic field. A coil with 50 turns produces a stronger electromagnet than a coil with 25 turns using the same current.

The Earth itself behaves like a giant magnet due to the movement of liquid iron in the core, which creates electric currents generating a magnetic field. This field helps migratory birds, fish, and animals navigate and protects life by blocking harmful particles from space.

Lifting electromagnets are strong electromagnets that can be attached to cranes. The crane operator controls the magnet by switching the current ON and OFF. When current is turned ON, the electromagnet lifts iron and steel objects; when current is switched OFF, the magnetic field disappears and the objects are released.
Lifting electromagnets are widely used in factories and scrap yards to move, lift, and sort heavy metal items efficiently and safely.

sections.section_title: 4.2 Does a Current-Carrying Wire Get Hot?

content.text: When electric current flows through any conductor, it faces opposition to its flow, known as resistance. Different conductors offer different levels of resistance to the flow of current. For example, a nichrome wire offers higher resistance compared to a copper wire of the same size and length.

The resistance in a conductor causes some electrical energy to be converted into heat energy. When an electric current passes through a conductor, it gets heated. This warming is known as the heating effect of electric current.

The amount of heat generated in a wire depends on several factors: (1) The material of the wire—different materials have different resistances; (2) The thickness (gauge) of the wire—thinner wires generate more heat; (3) The length of the wire—longer wires generate more heat; (4) The magnitude of the electric current—greater current generates more heat; (5) The duration for which current flows—longer duration produces more total heat.

When a nichrome wire is connected to a single cell and current flows for 30 seconds, the wire becomes warm. When the same nichrome wire is connected to a battery with 2 cells, the wire becomes much hotter for the same duration because the larger current generates more heat.

Many household appliances work on the principle of the heating effect of electric current. These devices contain a rod or a coil of wire called a heating element. In some appliances, this element is visible and can be seen glowing red hot.

items: Electric room heaters—used to provide warmth in homes and offices
Electric stoves and cooktops—used for cooking food
Immersion rods—used to heat water in containers
Hair dryers—used to dry and style hair
Incandescent lamp filaments—glow because they are heated by electric current

content.text: In incandescent lamps, electricity heats the filament to such a high temperature that it glows and emits light. In Grade 7, students learned how incandescent lamps work through practical activities.

The heating effect of electric current is useful in many everyday appliances. However, it can also cause problems. Overheating in appliances may cause damage to plugs and sockets where plastic parts may melt, or even lead to fires. Unnecessary heating in wires during transmission of electricity causes energy loss.

To prevent unnecessary heating in household switchboards, it is important to use appropriate wires, plugs, and sockets that are rated for the specified electric current of the connections. Household circuits have safety devices placed to minimise heating-related incidents.

Beyond household use, the heating effect of electric current has important industrial applications. In steel manufacturing industries, specially designed high-temperature furnaces use electric current to produce heat, which is then used to melt and recycle scrap steel into usable steel.

sections.section_title: 4.3 How Does a Battery Generate Electricity?

content.text: A Voltaic cell (also known as a Galvanic cell) is one of the earliest types of electric cells ever made. It contains two metal plates made of different materials (called electrodes) and a liquid called an electrolyte, all placed in a glass or plastic container.

The two metal plates (electrodes) are partly dipped in the electrolyte, which is usually a weak acid or a salt solution. A chemical reaction between the plates and the electrolyte produces electricity. When the circuit is connected, electric current flows from the positive terminal (cathode) through the circuit to the negative terminal (anode).
Over time, the chemicals get used up during the chemical reaction, and the cell stops working. It is then called 'dead' and cannot supply any more electricity.

The Voltaic cell was invented by Alessandro Volta based on observations by Luigi Galvani. Galvani noticed that a dead frog's leg kicked when touched with two different metals (copper and iron). Volta believed the electricity came from the metals, not the frog. He tested this by using saltwater-soaked paper instead of a frog's leg and still got an electric current, proving that the combination of metals and liquid generates electric current.

A Voltaic cell can be made using lemons, copper wires, and iron nails. The lemon juice acts as the electrolyte, while the copper and iron serve as electrodes. Multiple lemon cells can be connected in series to increase the voltage and power output. Common metal pairs for Voltaic cells include zinc/copper, zinc/silver, aluminium/copper, iron/copper, magnesium/copper, and lead/copper.

Voltaic cells were important discoveries, but they are not convenient for everyday use because they are messy and have liquid electrolyte. Dry cells are one of the most widely used electric cells today and are called 'dry' because the electrolyte is not a liquid but a thick moist paste.

The structure of a dry cell consists of: (1) A zinc container which acts as the negative terminal; (2) A carbon rod at the centre covered with a metal cap that acts as the positive terminal; (3) A thick paste-like electrolyte surrounding the carbon rod.

A dry cell is a single-use cell, meaning once it is used up, it has to be disposed of. The chemicals inside gradually deplete during use, and eventually the cell becomes 'dead' and unable to supply electricity.

Rechargeable batteries can be charged and recharged multiple times, allowing them to be reused indefinitely (until they wear out). This prevents wastage and saves money over time compared to single-use dry cells.

Many different kinds of rechargeable batteries are used for various applications: (1) Small batteries in watches and phones; (2) Batteries in laptops and tablets; (3) Larger batteries that run inverters and backup power systems; (4) Batteries that drive electric vehicles.

Rechargeable batteries do not last forever. After being charged and used many times, they slowly wear out, which is why phone batteries eventually require charging more frequently after a year or two of use.

Today, lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries are the most common type of rechargeable battery, found in almost all devices that use batteries. These batteries rely on special metals like lithium and cobalt, which are mined and processed in limited parts of the world. Scientists are working on the next big leap: solid-state batteries, which replace the liquid or paste-like electrolytes with solid materials. These future batteries would be much safer, charge faster, and last longer.
Improved rechargeable batteries are very important as the world moves toward developing environmentally friendly sources of electrical power.

Even when a battery stops working, it is not completely 'dead'. It could still contain hazardous materials like acids and metals such as lead, cadmium, nickel, or lithium, which may cause fires or be harmful for the environment if the battery is thrown in regular garbage.

Many materials used in batteries are valuable and could be recycled and reused. There are special 'e-waste' recycling facilities where used batteries can be disposed of safely. Recycling batteries is good for the planet and the people, as it prevents environmental contamination and conserves valuable resources.

glossary.definition: The phenomenon where an electric current flowing through a conductor produces a magnetic field around it.
A current-carrying coil that behaves as a magnet. Most practical electromagnets have an iron core to increase their strength.
The region around a magnet or current-carrying wire where its magnetic effect can be felt, detected by the deflection of a compass needle.
The generation of heat in a conductor due to the flow of electric current, caused by resistance to the flow of current.
The opposition that a conductor presents to the flow of electric current. Different materials and dimensions offer different resistances.
A wire made of nickel and chromium alloy that offers high resistance to electric current and is commonly used in heating appliances.
An early type of electric cell consisting of two different metal electrodes and an electrolyte, which generates electricity through chemical reactions.
The two metal plates in a Voltaic cell that are dipped in the electrolyte and participate in the chemical reaction to generate electricity.
A liquid (usually a weak acid or salt solution) or paste-like substance in a cell that enables chemical reactions between the electrodes to produce electricity.
A practical electric cell with a paste-like electrolyte instead of a liquid. It is portable and widely used, but can only be used once.
A battery that can be charged and discharged multiple times, allowing reuse over an extended period of time.
The most common type of modern rechargeable battery, used in phones, laptops, and electric vehicles because of its high energy density and reusability.
A strong electromagnet attached to cranes that can lift heavy metal objects by switching current ON and OFF.
The North and South poles of an electromagnet, similar to bar magnets. Unlike poles attract each other, and polarity can be reversed by changing current direction.
A rod or coil of wire in an electric heating appliance that generates heat when electric current flows through it.

summary_points.description: When electric current flows through a conductor, it produces a magnetic field around it. This magnetic effect was discovered by Hans Christian Oersted in 1820 and can be detected using a magnetic compass.

A current-carrying coil acts as an electromagnet. Its strength depends on the amount of current flowing through it, the number of turns in the coil, and whether it has an iron core. An electromagnet has two poles (North and South), and its polarity can be reversed by changing the current direction.

Electromagnets are used in lifting electromagnets for cranes in factories, electric bells, motors, fans, and loudspeakers. The Earth's magnetic field is caused by the movement of liquid iron in the core, which helps organisms navigate and protects life from harmful space particles.

Electric current flowing through a conductor generates heat due to resistance. The amount of heat depends on the material, thickness, length of the wire, and the magnitude and duration of current flow.

Many household appliances (heaters, stoves, irons, kettles, hair dryers) work by converting electrical energy to heat using a heating element. To prevent fire hazards, appropriate wires, plugs, and sockets rated for the specified current must be used.

A Voltaic cell generates electricity through chemical reactions between two different metal electrodes and an electrolyte. It was invented by Alessandro Volta and has two terminals (positive and negative). Once the chemicals are used up, the cell becomes 'dead'.

Dry cells use a paste-like electrolyte and can only be used once. Rechargeable batteries (like lithium-ion batteries) can be charged and reused many times, reducing waste and saving money, but they eventually wear out after repeated cycles.

Used batteries contain hazardous materials and valuable metals that should be recycled in special e-waste facilities, not thrown in regular garbage. Recycling batteries protects the environment and conserves valuable resources.

The discovery that electricity produces magnetism (and later that moving magnets produce electricity) is fundamental to modern technology. This connection powers motors, generators, transformers, and many other essential devices.
